articles.title: Trump’s White House ballroom fight turns into a test of presidential power
articles.summary: A construction project that began as a flashy presidential makeover has become a live legal and political battle over Congress, the courts and how far a White House occupant can go in reshaping the historic grounds.

articles.title: Tariffs, Inflation and a Late-Cycle Economy: Why April’s Data Still Leave Washington Guessing
articles.summary: Fresh federal data show consumer spending holding up, inflation still running above the Fed’s target and the labor market cooling only gradually—just as tariff uncertainty threatens to complicate the path forward.

articles.title: OpenAI’s push into safety, enterprise and influence shows AI’s new center of gravity
articles.summary: In a single week, OpenAI moved on child safety, startup investment, a safety fellowship and a media acquisition, underscoring how the AI race in 2026 is now as much about trust, governance and reach as it is about model quality.

articles.title: Earth’s night lights are flickering, and the map of civilization is getting stranger
articles.summary: A new Nature study shows that artificial light on Earth is no longer simply expanding outward. It is brightening, dimming and shifting in bursts—especially across Asia—offering a rare, data-rich glimpse of how urbanization, electrification and instability are reshaping the planet after dark.

articles.title: Inflation Has Eased, But the Fed’s Next Move Is Still Complicated
articles.summary: March CPI and PCE data point to cooling price pressures, but sticky services inflation, tariff uncertainty and a still-tight labor market are keeping the Federal Reserve cautious ahead of its April 28-29 meeting.

articles.title: Artemis II’s moon flyby is over. What the mission has already changed for science.
articles.summary: NASA’s first crewed lunar mission in more than half a century has returned four astronauts to Earth, but its real significance may be what it teaches about deep-space flight, lunar operations, and the next phase of the Artemis program.

articles.title: AI’s new center of gravity is enterprise control, not just model bragging rights
articles.summary: OpenAI, Google and Anthropic have spent the first weeks of spring 2026 pushing a clear message: the AI race is shifting from raw model launches to the harder work of wiring agents into real businesses, with security, context, and governance now part of the product itself.

articles.title: NASA’s Swift spacecraft is getting a rescue mission in orbit
articles.summary: A 21-year-old gamma-ray observatory is losing altitude faster than expected. NASA is betting that a commercial robot can save it — and prove a new kind of space tug is viable.

articles.title: U.S.-Iran ceasefire talks collapse, leaving the Strait of Hormuz and a wider regional war on edge
articles.summary: After two days of talks in Pakistan ended without a deal, Washington and Tehran are back to brinkmanship over Iran’s nuclear program, a fragile ceasefire, and the prospect of confrontation in one of the world’s most strategic waterways.

articles.title: The redistricting war that could decide the 2026 House: Texas holds, Missouri follows, and the map fight widens
articles.summary: A Supreme Court ruling on Texas, a Missouri court victory for Republicans, and the broader collapse of stable redistricting rules have turned the 2026 House map into a high-stakes political weapon.
